# Checklist — Software Requirements (SWEBOK v4, Chapter 1)

> Reference checklist derived from **SWEBOK Guide v4.0, Chapter 01 — Software Requirements** (pp. 1-1 → 1-24).
> Each row defines one verification check on a software requirements set / process.
> **Ordered by severity of a *missing* check** (Critical → High → Medium → Low). Missing a Critical check is likely to cause project failure, safety/security incidents, or massive rework; missing a Low check is a hygiene/optimization gap.
>
> Severity scale:
>
> - **Critical** — absence directly threatens correctness, safety, security, contractual compliance, or causes cascading rework.
> - **High** — absence very likely produces ambiguity, scope blowups, or expensive defects late in the lifecycle.
> - **Medium** — absence degrades maintainability, communication, or efficiency; typically reworkable.
> - **Low** — hygiene/tooling/process-improvement gap with limited near-term impact.

## Checklist

| ID | Check name | Reference (§ / page) | Severity | What must be verified | Concrete example (✅ success / ❌ failure) |
|----|------------|----------------------|----------|----------------------|--------------------------------------------|
| SR-001 | Safety & security requirements addressed | §1.7 Quality of Service Constraints; §7.6 (CIA) `[1-4, 1-5, 1-20]` | Critical | Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability and safety requirements are explicitly captured (not "overlooked"). Security KA is consulted; ISO/IEC 25010 quality characteristics are considered. | ✅ Spec includes "All PII fields must be encrypted at rest with AES-256 and in transit with TLS 1.3; admin actions on user accounts must be logged with actor, timestamp, before/after values, retained 365 days." ❌ Spec only describes business flows; no statement about authn/authz, encryption, audit logging, or data classification. |
| SR-002 | Requirements set is complete (boundary, exception, security) | §3.1 Basic Requirements Analysis `[1-8]` | Critical | The collection adequately addresses boundary conditions, exception conditions, and security needs — not just the happy path. | ✅ For "withdraw cash": scenarios for sufficient balance, insufficient balance, disabled card, ATM out of cash, network timeout, max daily limit reached. ❌ Only "Given enough balance, when user withdraws X, dispense X" — no error/edge scenarios. |
| SR-003 | Each requirement is unambiguous | §3.1 `[1-8]`; ambiguity is one of the two primary problems `[1-1]` | Critical | Each requirement is interpretable in only one way; no vague modifiers ("fast", "user-friendly", "appropriate"). Prefer ATDD/BDD or model-based forms where possible. | ✅ "The login API shall respond within 300 ms at the 95th percentile under 1000 concurrent users." ❌ "The system shall respond quickly even under heavy load." |
| SR-004 | Requirements set is feasible | §3.1 `[1-8]` | Critical | A viable, cost-effective solution can be built within the agreed cost, schedule, staffing and other constraints. Not an aggregate of mutually impossible demands. | ✅ Estimation shows the prioritized scope fits the 6-month budget with a 15% buffer. ❌ Spec mandates sub-50ms global response time, 99.999% availability, single-region deployment, and a $20k cloud budget — three of these contradict. |
| SR-005 | Requirements set is internally consistent | §3.1 `[1-8]` | Critical | No requirement conflicts with any other in the set. Conflicts have been surfaced and resolved (or marked as variants — see §3.4). | ✅ A traceability/consistency review found two conflicting statements about refund eligibility window; resolved to "30 days" and recorded. ❌ One section says "refunds allowed within 14 days", another says "refunds allowed within 60 days"; both shipped to dev. |
| SR-006 | Stakeholder analysis performed | §2.1 Requirements Sources `[1-6, 1-7]` | Critical | All relevant stakeholder classes (clients, customers, users incl. user classes, SMEs, ops, support, regulators, special interest groups, negatively-impacted parties, developers) are identified and consulted; coverage is not biased toward the loudest. | ✅ Stakeholder map lists 11 classes including "regulator (GDPR)" and "support tier-1"; each has a named representative and elicitation record. ❌ Requirements come exclusively from the product manager and one sales contact; ops, support, and regulators were never interviewed. |
| SR-007 | Acceptance criteria defined for each functional requirement | §4.3 Acceptance Criteria-Based Spec; §4.5 attributes `[1-12, 1-13, 1-14]` | Critical | Each functional requirement (or user story) has explicit, executable acceptance criteria (ATDD test cases or BDD Given/When/Then scenarios). | ✅ Story "withdraw cash" has 4 BDD scenarios (sufficient balance, insufficient balance, card disabled, ATM empty) ready as acceptance tests. ❌ Story says only "User can withdraw cash"; no Given/When/Then, no measurable pass condition. |
| SR-008 | Change control process is in place | §6.2 Requirements Change Control `[1-17]` | Critical | An explicit process exists with: change request mechanism, optional impact analysis, accountable decision-maker, stakeholder notification, tracking to closure. (In Agile this manifests via the prioritized backlog.) | ✅ JIRA "Change Request" workflow: submit → impact analysis → CCB decision → notify stakeholders → linked implementation issue tracked to release. ❌ Devs change scope mid-sprint based on a Slack message from the PM; no record, no impact analysis, ops finds out at deploy. |
| SR-009 | Functional vs nonfunctional requirements are separated | §1.4, §1.5, §1.8 Why Categorize `[1-4, 1-5]` | High | Requirements are classified; the "Perfect Technology Filter" is used to decide (would the requirement still be needed on infinite/free hardware?). Categories enable focused review by the right experts. | ✅ Spec organized in "Functional", "Technology Constraints", "Quality of Service" sections; business expert reviews only "Functional", ops reviews only constraints. ❌ Single flat list intermixing "User can edit profile", "Runs on Postgres 16", and "p95 latency < 200 ms"; reviewers skim past whatever isn't theirs. |
| SR-010 | Each requirement is testable / quantified | §3.1 `[1-8]` | High | Compliance or noncompliance can be clearly demonstrated. Vague adjectives are replaced by measurable criteria. | ✅ "Search results page renders ≤ 800 ms at p95 for catalogs ≤ 1M items." ❌ "The system should be responsive and scale well." |
| SR-011 | QoS constraints have fail and perfection points | §3.2 Economics of QoS `[1-8, 1-9]` | High | For each QoS constraint (response time, throughput, accuracy, reliability, scalability...) the **fail point** (below which there is no further loss of value) and **perfection point** (above which there is no additional value) are stated, ideally with a cost-effective target between them. | ✅ "Login latency: fail point 2s (users abandon), perfection point 100ms (no perceived benefit below), target 300ms p95." ❌ "Login must be fast" — no target, no fail point; team over-optimizes to 50ms at 3× cost. |
| SR-012 | External consistency with source materials verified | §3.1 `[1-8]` | High | No requirement conflicts with referenced source documents (system requirements, standards, regulations, contracts, prior versions). | ✅ Traceability matrix shows each requirement maps to the relevant clause of the contract and ISO 27001 control; conflicts flagged in review. ❌ Spec says "store data for 90 days"; underlying GDPR-derived corporate policy says max 30 days. Nobody noticed. |
| SR-013 | Requirements validation reviews from multiple perspectives | §5.1 Requirements Reviews `[1-15, 1-16]` | High | Reviews include (a) clients/customers/users, (b) requirements specialists, (c) downstream architects/designers/developers. A checklist / definition of done guides reviewers. | ✅ Each spec has 3 reviewer sign-offs (business owner, RA lead, tech lead) against a published review checklist. ❌ Only the author and PM read the spec before dev starts; architecture finds it unimplementable in sprint 2. |
| SR-014 | Conflict resolution approach is documented | §3.4 Addressing Conflict `[1-10]` | High | Conflicts are surfaced; resolution is either negotiated with stakeholders (with traceability back to the customer) or handled via product family development (invariant vs variant requirements). Unilateral engineer decisions are avoided. | ✅ Decision log: "Conflict on units (°C vs °F) resolved via product-family variant; both supported; decision approved by both client reps on 2026-04-12." ❌ Dev silently picked °C; US customer rejects UAT. |
| SR-015 | Requirements traceability established (backward & forward) | §7.3 Requirements Tracing `[1-18, 1-19]` | High | Each requirement is traceable backward to source documentation and forward to design elements, test cases, and (where relevant) user-manual sections. Used both for accounting and for change-impact analysis. | ✅ RTM links REQ-101 → SYS-12, design DD-204, tests T-441/T-442, user-manual §3.4. Change to SYS-12 immediately shows the affected footprint. ❌ No traceability; a regulatory clause changes and nobody knows which features or tests it impacts. |
| SR-016 | Scope matching against cost/schedule/staffing | §6.3 Scope Matching `[1-17]` | High | The requirements scope is reconciled with project constraints, ideally in quantitative functional-size units, before commitment. | ✅ Estimated 320 FP vs 280 FP capacity → 12 lowest-priority items deferred to v1.1. ❌ All 320 FP committed for the same date; team burns out and ships late and incomplete. |
| SR-017 | Requirements are atomic (one decision each) | §3.1 `[1-8]` | High | Each requirement encodes a single decision; compound "and / or" requirements are decomposed. | ✅ Split into REQ-12a "User can reset password via email" and REQ-12b "User can reset password via SMS". ❌ "User can reset password via email and SMS and security questions, except for admin accounts which use IT helpdesk only" — one row, impossible to track partial completion or test independently. |
| SR-018 | Requirements represent true stakeholder needs (5-whys) | §3.1 `[1-8]` | High | Each requirement has been challenged to ensure it states the *real problem*, not a presumed solution; the 5-whys (or equivalent) is applied when a "solution" is stated. | ✅ "Add an Excel export button" → 5-whys → root need: "monthly reconciliation"; solved by an automated reconciliation report instead. ❌ Team builds the Excel export; users still spend 3 days/month reconciling manually. |
| SR-019 | Configuration management applied to requirements | §4 intro `[1-11]` | High | The requirements documents (and where practical individual requirements) are versioned, baselined, and managed under CM, supported by a requirements management tool. | ✅ Requirements stored in a tool with version history, baselines per release, and audit trail. ❌ Requirements live in an unversioned Word file on a shared drive; multiple "final_v2_real_FINAL.docx" copies exist. |
| SR-020 | Multiple appropriate elicitation techniques used | §2.2 Common Elicitation Techniques `[1-7, 1-8]` | High | Elicitation combines several techniques fit for the stakeholder class (interviews, workshops, JAD/JRP, prototyping, observation, document analysis, scenarios, design thinking, standards review, etc.). Reliance on a single passive channel is avoided. | ✅ Mix: 8 SME interviews + 2 JAD workshops + on-site observation + competitive benchmark + ISO 25010 review. ❌ A single questionnaire sent by email; 30% response rate; team treats it as complete input. |
| SR-021 | System vs software requirements distinction made | §1.9 `[1-5, 1-6]` | Medium | Where the software is part of a larger system, system requirements and software requirements are distinguished, and software requirements derived from system requirements are explicitly identified. | ✅ For an autonomous vehicle module: spec has "SYS-001 vehicle stops within 5 m at 50 km/h" and "SW-014 brake-control module shall publish stop command within 20 ms". ❌ One mixed list; later impossible to tell which clauses are allocated to software vs hardware. |
| SR-022 | Derived requirements identified and labeled | §1.10 Derived Requirements `[1-6]` | Medium | Requirements imposed by internal teams (architecture decisions cascading to sub-teams, etc.) are tagged as derived, with their originating decision recorded. | ✅ REQ-D-031 (derived from architecture decision ADR-007: pipes-and-filters) is visible to the filter sub-team. ❌ Filter sub-team treats an architect's choice as an external client requirement; later push-back from the client surprises everyone. |
| SR-023 | Technology constraints explicitly listed | §1.6 Technology Constraints `[1-4]` | Medium | Mandated or prohibited technologies/platforms/languages/DBs are explicitly enumerated, not implicit. | ✅ "Mandatory: Java 21 LTS, PostgreSQL 16, Kubernetes. Prohibited: native pointers, AGPL libraries." ❌ Team assumes "we always use Postgres"; mid-project a stakeholder mandates SQL Server. |
| SR-024 | Requirements prioritized | §7.2 Requirements Prioritization `[1-17, 1-18]` | Medium | Priorities are assigned considering value, dissatisfaction (Kano), cost, maintenance cost, technical risk, and risk of non-use. Communicated via a clear scheme (MoSCoW, 1..10, ordered list). | ✅ Backlog uses MoSCoW; top 50 items ordered; Kano analysis attached for the v1 scope. ❌ All 200 items marked "High"; team picks whatever is most fun first. |
| SR-025 | Requirements stability / volatility assessed | §7.4 Stability and Volatility `[1-19]` | Medium | Each (or each group of) requirement is annotated with expected volatility so the design can isolate volatile areas. | ✅ "REQ-22 tax-free account types — VOLATILE (legislation pending); design behind a strategy interface." ❌ Volatile tax rules hard-coded throughout the codebase; first legislative change requires a multi-sprint refactor. |
| SR-026 | Requirements scrubbing performed | §6.1 Requirements Scrubbing `[1-16, 1-17]` | Medium | Out-of-scope, low-ROI, and low-importance requirements are removed; unnecessarily complex ones are simplified — before committing to build. | ✅ Pre-validation scrub removed 18 items (out of scope or low ROI); 5 simplified; saved ~40 SP. ❌ Everything elicited went straight into the build backlog; 30% of delivered features are never used. |
| SR-027 | Additional attributes recorded per requirement | §4.5 Additional Attributes `[1-14, 1-15]` | Medium | Useful per-requirement attributes are populated where relevant: tag, description, rationale, source, related use case, type, dependencies, conflicts, acceptance criteria, priority, stability, common/variant, supporting materials, change history. | ✅ Every REQ row has tag, source, rationale, priority, acceptance criteria, change history. ❌ Rows have only an ID and a one-line statement; nobody can answer "why is this here?" 6 months later. |
| SR-028 | Iterative requirements process used | §7.1 Iterative Nature `[1-17]` | Medium | The team alternates breadth-expanding and depth-expanding passes rather than attempting a single big-bang elicitation. | ✅ Discovery in two breadth waves + three depth waves on prioritized epics; new requirements continuously folded in. ❌ Single 6-week requirements phase, then "frozen"; reality forces 40% rework during build. |
| SR-029 | Audience analysis informs documentation format | §4 intro `[1-11]` | Medium | Documentation choices (form, depth, navigation) are based on identified consumers and their information needs. | ✅ Spec has an exec summary for the steering committee, a model-based section for devs, and a glossary for QA. ❌ Single 200-page Word document; executives skip it, devs ctrl-F it, QA gives up. |
| SR-030 | Use stakeholder vocabulary (no inappropriate jargon) | §3.1 `[1-8]` | Medium | Requirements use the stakeholders' domain vocabulary so non-engineer reviewers can validate them. | ✅ Banking spec uses "deposit", "withdrawal", "passbook entry" rather than "POST /transactions". ❌ Spec uses ORM table names and HTTP verbs; the business sponsor can't validate it. |
| SR-031 | Acceptable to all stakeholders | §3.1 `[1-8]` | Medium | Each stakeholder class has formally accepted (or registered objections to) the requirements that affect them. | ✅ Sign-off matrix: 5 stakeholder classes × N requirement groups, all green or with linked objections being resolved. ❌ No sign-off; ops discovers at UAT that the on-call rotation impact was never agreed. |
| SR-032 | Concise — no extraneous content | §3.1 `[1-8]` | Medium | The requirements set contains no redundant, decorative, or off-topic content that hides the real requirements. | ✅ 120 atomic requirements, each one unique and necessary. ❌ 400 rows including marketing prose, repeated rationale, three near-duplicate statements of the same rule. |
| SR-033 | Use of incremental vs comprehensive spec strategy declared | §4.6 Incremental and Comprehensive Spec `[1-15]` | Low | The project explicitly chooses (and documents) whether each release ships an incremental delta spec, a comprehensive spec, or a hybrid; readers know how far back to look for the complete picture. | ✅ "Major releases (x.0) are comprehensive; minor releases (x.y) are incremental deltas referencing the last major baseline." ❌ Inconsistent — some releases ship deltas, some full specs, with no rule; maintainers must read N years of files to assemble the truth. |
| SR-034 | Formal analysis considered for high-integrity portions | §3.3 Formal Analysis `[1-9, 1-10]` | Low | For high-integrity / safety-critical components, the team has explicitly considered formal methods (Z, VDM, SDL, model checking, theorem proving) and either applied them or recorded the decision not to. | ✅ For the brake controller subsystem, the team formally verified deadlock-freedom using a model checker. ❌ Safety-critical control logic written from natural-language requirements only; no formal reasoning attempted, no rationale recorded. |
| SR-035 | Simulation / execution of formal models for validation | §5.2 Simulation and Execution `[1-16]` | Low | Where a model-based or formal spec exists, simulation or execution is offered to stakeholders as a validation channel instead of (or in addition to) document review. | ✅ Stakeholders walk through 12 simulated scenarios on the state model; they sign off on observed behavior. ❌ Model exists but nobody runs it; stakeholders re-read text and miss two transition bugs. |
| SR-036 | Prototyping used for unclear UI / dynamic behavior | §5.3 Prototyping `[1-16]` | Low | Where natural-language descriptions are insufficient (typically interactive UI or unclear dynamic behavior), a prototype is built to surface engineer assumptions. Cosmetic-distraction risk is managed (low-fidelity unless deliberate). | ✅ A low-fi clickable prototype validated the wizard flow; 3 misinterpretations caught before construction. ❌ Team builds the wizard from a paragraph of prose; users reject the flow at UAT, full rebuild needed. |
| SR-037 | Requirements size is measured | §7.5 Measuring Requirements `[1-19]` | Low | Functional Size Measurement (FSM) or story points (or equivalent) is used to size requirements, supporting estimation, change sizing, and ratios for other metrics. | ✅ Each epic carries an FP count; cost and schedule estimates are derived from it. ❌ "T-shirt sizes by gut feel"; estimates miss by 2–3× and there is no way to compare across releases. |
| SR-038 | Requirements process quality and improvement tracked | §7.6 Process Quality and Improvement `[1-19, 1-20]` | Low | The team measures the requirements process (e.g., defect leakage from requirements, churn, cycle time), benchmarks, plans and implements improvements; CIA/security improvement is included. | ✅ Quarterly retro of requirements metrics; two improvement actions per quarter; CIA control gaps tracked separately. ❌ No process metrics; the same elicitation gaps reappear release after release. |
| SR-039 | A requirements management tool is in use | §8.1 Requirements Management Tools `[1-20]` | Low | A purpose-built tool stores attributes, supports tracing, generates documents, and enforces change control — not just a spreadsheet. | ✅ Requirements live in Jama / DOORS / Polarion (or equivalent) with role-based access and history. ❌ Requirements in a flat Excel file emailed around; tracing is manual; multiple divergent copies exist. |
| SR-040 | Requirements modeling tool used where applicable | §8.2 Requirements Modeling Tools `[1-20]` | Low | Where model-based specs are appropriate, a tool supports creating/modifying/publishing them and (ideally) provides static analysis (syntax, completeness, consistency) or simulation. | ✅ UML/SysML models in Enterprise Architect with consistency rules enforced. ❌ Diagrams drawn ad hoc in slides; copies drift from the text spec; no validation. |
| SR-041 | Mechanical test-case generation considered | §8.3 Functional Test Case Generation Tools `[1-20]` | Low | Where the spec language allows (BDD, state models, formal specs), a tool is used to derive at least the test inputs mechanically. | ✅ BDD scenarios auto-converted into executable tests in CI; state-model transitions produce positive/negative cases. ❌ Hundreds of test cases hand-written from BDD scenarios, with predictable drift between specs and tests. |
